entry.title: Natural Language Automation for SAP Datasphere with GitHub Copilot
entry.content:
If you work with SAP Datasphere, you know the official CLI is powerful but not always easy to use. Remembering exact commands, building JSON payloads, converting storage to bytes, getting user roles right, and being careful with deletes takes a lot of time, especially when creating new feature spaces, developing new artifacts, auditing access, or running task chains.

A while ago I experimented with the SAP Data Intelligence Command-Line Client (vctl) and Monitoring Query API to automate parts of the system management and monitoring. It helped a bit, but the capabilities felt quite limited.

Now that SAP Datasphere and Business Data Cloud are gaining real traction, I decided to build something more practical that I could actually use every day.

That’s why I created datasphere-copilot - a custom GitHub Copilot agent that lets you manage SAP Datasphere using natural language instead of dealing with CLI commands and JSON payloads.

High‑level architecture of the datasphere‑copilot agent.

What the Agent Can Do Today

Instead of switching between documentation and terminal, you can simply say things like:

  • “Create a new feature space called PROJECT_X_FINANCE with 10 GB quota and description for our current implementation project”
  • “List all users in the tenant and show their assigned spaces and roles”
  • “Check current space quotas for all spaces”
  • “Deploy a local table named SALES_FACT with columns order_id, amount, and order_date”
  • “Run the task chain DAILY_ETL in space ANALYTICS”

These requests are common for architects and leads who need to quickly spin up environments, audit access rights, or keep multiple projects moving. Since the agent runs commands directly in the terminal, its output can easily feed into custom agentic orchestration for task chains and deployments.

Example of a complex, multi‑step SAP Datasphere operation handled from a single prompt. The instruction to assign "all developers" resulted in the agent filtering Sandbox users by role before assigning them to the new project space.

How It’s Built

The agent is designed to be lightweight and easy to extend. Earlier, I built a complex regex-based intent mapper that didn’t use any LLM. While it handled a well-defined set of commands, it was a bit fragile and harder to manage. With today’s reliable language models, maintaining a strictly rule-based approach no longer makes much sense.

The current design focuses on clarity and extensibility instead:

  • One central agent file that orchestrates the workflow
  • 9 focused skill files (detailed playbooks) covering spaces, modeling objects (16+ types), task chains, users & roles, and more
  • Automatic skill discovery - adding new skills is straightforward

Safety was a priority - read operations run immediately, create/update show full results, and destructive actions (like delete) always require explicit confirmation. Credentials stay protected in the .env file.

Performance with Different Models

I’ve tested this agent with several models. In my experience, Claude Sonnet 4.6 and GPT-5.3-Codex delivered the most consistent results for this type of code generation.

When choosing your Copilot model, keep in mind that accuracy can vary, especially with complex commands and payloads. It’s also worth tracking your premium request quota, as different models and reasoning settings can significantly increase costs.

Future Improvements

Here are a few things I plan to work on next:

  • Multitenant support - to work across multiple tenants and handle cross-tenant references more easily
  • Broader skill coverage - adding instructions for the remaining development artifact types
  • REST API + OData integration - unlock more capabilities by extending the agent to also use SAP’s official REST APIs and OData services alongside the CLI
  • Team of Agents - introducing specialized personas so you can have a “development agent”, “administration agent”, and “security agent” working together in different areas

Try It Yourself

If you’re using SAP Datasphere and looking to speed up common workflows, the project is open source and available on GitHub: https://github.com/dsteffanov/datasphere-copilot 

Prerequisites: VS Code (1.99+ or Insiders), GitHub Copilot license, Node.js 18+, and the official @sap/datasphere-cli for SAP Datasphere.

Quick start in 3 steps:

  1. Clone the repo and open it in VS Code
  2. Copy .env.example to .env and fill in your tenant credentials
  3. Open Copilot Chat in Agent mode, select the datasphere-copilot agent, and start chatting

Star the repo if you find it useful, open an issue for feature requests, or submit a PR to add new skills.

Final Thoughts

I know SAP Joule is already quite well integrated into the modern SAP stack, but I haven’t tried it for these kinds of tasks yet. Many dev teams are exploring different AI coding assistants (Claude Code, ChatGPT Codex, etc.) depending on their organization’s licensing and strategy. Since this agent is built for GitHub Copilot, I hope it will be useful for those who already have or are adopting Copilot.

On a separate note, I’m still evaluating whether introducing an MCP server makes sense for this particular agent. While it could improve tool discovery and make interactions smoother with Claude models, it would also add extra infrastructure. For now, I prefer keeping the solution as lightweight and easy to adopt as possible… until I hit a limit.

This is still very much a work in progress, but I’m already using it daily and it saves me a noticeable amount of time. If you give it a try, I’d genuinely appreciate your feedback - especially ideas for new skills or real‑world use cases.

entry.title: Activating Document Grounding with Joule in SAP BTP
entry.content:
Dear community,

I have a question about activating Document Grounding in SAP BTP: Do AI Core units, Joule, and Document Grounding need to be in the same global account or the same subaccount?

In the prerequisites, the documentation says: “You’ve added the entitlements for Document Grounding to the subaccount where you’ve subscribed to Joule,” and at the same time: “You’ve purchased the AI Unit SKU (8019164) and AI units. After you’ve done so, the entitlements for Document Grounding are automatically added to your global account.”

Does this mean that all three - AI units, Joule, and the Document Grounding instance - must be in the same global account and subaccount? Or are there any exceptions?

entry.title: User Filtering for Repository Synchronization in SAP Identity Access Governance
entry.content:
Introduction:

In the Standard Edition of SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance (IAG), subscription consumption is calculated using the Resources metric. This metric represents the number of unique users, including technical users, synchronized from on‑premise and/or cloud systems into SAP IAG.

More information available on: SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance - License Metric 

3262845 - How to monitor IAG usage? 

In many customers’ landscapes, not all users maintained in target systems require identity governance. Test users, technical users, and generic accounts typically do not participate in provisioning, access risk analysis, or access certification. Synchronizing such users into SAP IAG may unnecessarily increase subscription consumption without delivering business value.

To address this, SAP IAG provides user filtering capabilities during repository synchronization, enabling customers to limit synchronization to governance‑relevant users only. This blog outlines the available filtering options across common SAP IAG integration scenarios.

Integration Scenarios in SAP IAG Standard Edition

Depending on the target application, SAP IAG supports the following integration models:

  1. On‑premise SAP ABAP systems
    Connected via SAP Cloud Connector
    Examples: SAP ECC, SAP S/4HANA (On‑Premise)
  2. SAP cloud applications integrated using SAP Identity Provisioning Service (IPS)
    Connected through a proxy system
    Examples: SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS), SAP Ariba, SAP Analytics Cloud
  3. SAP cloud applications integrated directly using SAP BTP destinations
    Examples: SAP Concur, SAP IBP, SAP Marketing Cloud, SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

Each integration model offers distinct options for filtering users during repository synchronization.

1. User Filtering for Applications Integrated via SAP IPS Proxy

For applications integrated through SAP Identity Provisioning Service (IPS), user filtering is implemented using transformation rules in the proxy system. These rules determine which users are synchronized into SAP IAG during repository sync.

Example: Filtering Users from SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS)

In this scenario, users are filtered based on IAS user group membership:

  • A group named Z_SAP_IAG_TEST is maintained in SAP IAS.
  • An IPS transformation rule is configured to synchronize only users assigned to this group.
  • During repository synchronization, only users belonging to this group are synced into SAP IAG, while all other IAS users are excluded.

This approach ensures that only relevant users are onboarded into SAP IAG.

Currently there are 16 users assigned to this group in SAP IAS.

On execution of repository object sync in SAP IAG for SAP IAS, only 16 users got synchronized.

Sample Transformation Filter Expressions

Filters can be defined using various user attributes, depending on the application. Below are few examples:

SAP IAS

ias.user.filter = userName eq "john.doe"
ias.user.filter = userName co "admin"
ias.user.filter = groups.display eq "Finance_Team" or groups.display eq "HR_Team"

SAP Analytics Cloud

sac.user.filter = name.familyName eq "Smith" and addresses.country eq "Germany"
sac.user.filter = userName sw "SAP_"

SAP Ariba

ariba.applications.user.filter = userName eq "SmithJ"
ariba.applications.user.filter = name.familyName eq "Johnson" and addresses.country eq "USA"
ariba.applications.user.filter = userName sw "PROC_"

These filters allow precise control over which users are synchronized based on naming patterns, attributes, or group assignments.

2. User Filtering for Applications Integrated via BTP Destination or Cloud Connector

Some SAP cloud applications do not require an IPS proxy and can be integrated directly using SAP BTP destinations.

Similarly, on‑premise ABAP systems first establish connectivity through SAP Cloud Connector, followed by destination configuration in the BTP subaccount.

After integration, each target system is represented as an Application in SAP IAG under Administration.

Using the User Filter Option in SAP IAG

SAP IAG provides a User Filter option at the application level:

  • Filters are defined using attributes such as User ID, User Group, or User Type (availability depends on the application).
  • Currently, SAP IAG supports exclude‑only filtering.
  • Users matching the exclusion criteria are not synchronized into SAP IAG during repository sync.

 

 Example: Excluding Users from SAP Concur

In this example:

  • Specific Concur user IDs are configured in the exclusion filter.
  • Multiple exclusion conditions can be added.
  • During repository synchronization, excluded users are no longer synchronized into SAP IAG.

This approach is effective for excluding technical or generic users across recurring synchronizations.

On‑Premise ABAP Systems (ECC / S/4HANA)

For SAP ECC and SAP S/4HANA (On‑Premise):

  • Exclusion filters can be applied using User ID, User Group and User Type.
  • For advanced requirements, SAP provides an enhancement option via enhancement spot in SIAG_USER_GET_LIST function module and BADI implementation.

Relevant SAP Note:2802767 - Enhancement for filtering users in SAP ECC and S/4 HANA OP sync to IAG 

3. User Filtering for SAP SuccessFactors

SAP SuccessFactors offers a dedicated filtering mechanism using application parameters in SAP IAG.

Configuration Details

  • Navigate to Administration -> Configuration application in SAP IAG
    • Configuration Group: SUCCESSFACTORS_SYNC
    • Parameter Name: USER_FILTER
    • Parameter Value: condition for different attributes and values.

Filtering is based on attributes exposed by the SuccessFactors OData API.

Examples of Filter Conditions

1. Synchronize only active employees:

Parameter value: status eq 't'

2. Synchronize only permanent employees:

empInfo/JobInfoNav/employmentType eq '3637'

If the attribute is not the top-level service, OData navigation paths must be used. Here the navigation is empInfo-> JobInfoNav- > employmentType

 Multiple conditions can be combined using the ‘and’ operator.

Refer SAP document for more information: Application Parameters | SAP help portal

 

Conclusion

User filtering during repository synchronization is a key capability in SAP Identity Access Governance that helps customers:

  • Optimize subscription consumption
  • Avoid onboarding non-governance‑relevant users
  • Maintain a lean and efficient identity landscape

By using IPS transformation rules, application‑level exclusion filters, enhancement options for on‑premise systems, and SuccessFactors application parameters, customers can align SAP IAG synchronization precisely with their governance requirements.

entry.title: Understanding Log Levels in SAP Cloud Integration (CPI)
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Cloud Integration (CPI) provides different log levels to monitor and troubleshoot iFlows effectively. Many beginners find it confusing to understand when to use Info, Error, Debug, and Trace and how each level affects message visibility.

In this blog, I will explain each log level in a simple way along with practical usage.

What Are Log Levels in SAP CPI?

Log levels define how much information CPI captures while processing a message.

Choosing the correct log level helps you:

  • Monitor message execution
  • Troubleshoot issues efficiently
  • Avoid unnecessary performance overhead

Log Configuration in CPI

Below is the Log Configuration screen where you select the log level before running an iFlow:

 

 

 

 

 

Explanation of Each Log Level:

None         

  • No logs are recorded
  • Used for high-volume or performance-critical scenarios
  • Not recommended for troubleshooting 

Info (Default)

Captures basic events:

  • Message start
  •  Message completion
  • High-level status

Suitable for regular monitoring

Recommended for production

Error

  • Logs only when an error occurs

  • Helps identify failures without excessive logging

  • Useful in production environments

Debug    

  • Provides step-by-step execution details

  • Does NOT capture full payload

  • Helps understand flow behavior

Trace    

Captures complete details:

Payload before and after each step

  • Mapping output
  • Router conditions
  • Splitter behavior

Best for deep troubleshooting

Should be used temporarily (can impact performance)

How CPI Internally Behaves Based on Log Levels

When an iFlow runs, CPI behaves differently depending on the selected log level:

  • For Info / Error → Only summary logs are stored
  • For Debug → Step-level information is captured
  • For Trace → Full payload snapshots are stored at every step

This directly affects:

  • Message Processing Logs (MPL)
  • Trace log visibility
  • System performance

Conclusion

Log Levels are a fundamental part of SAP CPI monitoring and debugging.

Understanding when to use each level helps you:

  • Diagnose issues faster
  • Improve performance
  • Avoid unnecessary logging

Note: This blog is based on my personal learning and hands-on experience with SAP Cloud Integration (CPI).

#SAPCPI #SAPIntegration #BTP #Logging

@Bibekananda1231 

@Souravraj_Maitra

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition: CPFH Sales Kits in Consignment
entry.content:
Introduction

With release 2402, support for Sales Kits of type CPFH in consignment processing was delivered.

At first sight, this may look like a limited scope enhancement: allowing a Sales Kit to be used in consignment scenarios. In reality, the change is much more significant. The real requirement was not just order entry support, but a process model that preserves the commercial meaning of the kit while still allowing the system to execute the logistics and inventory steps on component level.

This is exactly where CPFH becomes relevant.

In a CPFH Sales Kit, the processing logic is clearly split:

  • the main item carries the commercial meaning and pricing
  • the subitems carry the delivery- and inventory-relevant execution

This split is particularly important in consignment processing, because consignment is not just a sales-document-driven scenario. It is a stock-oriented business process with distinct follow-up steps:

  • Consignment Fill-Up
  • Consignment Issue
  • Consignment Pick-Up
  • Consignment Return

For a normal material, these steps are straightforward. For a Sales Kit, they are not. The customer orders one commercial object, but the system needs to execute on multiple physical components.

This blog explains how CPFH Sales Kits are processed in consignment, what the business meaning of the relevant item categories is, and how the solution integrates into the standard consignment execution framework.

Business Background

Among the different Sales Kit types, CPFH is especially suitable for consignment because it already reflects the separation that consignment processing needs:

  • commercial meaning on header level
  • execution meaning on component level

From a business perspective, this allows the system to preserve the kit as the object the customer sees and buys.

From an execution perspective, it allows the system to process the components that are physically delivered, consumed, picked up, or returned.

Without such a split, a kit might still look correct in the sales document, but the downstream process would become inconsistent. The document would show one object, while the actual stock movements would need another.

The core design principle is therefore simple:

The header keeps the kit semantics. The subitems carry the consignment execution semantics.

Scope Delivered in 2402

The current delivery scope focuses on Sales Kits of type CPFH for the following consignment process steps:

  1. Consignment Fill-Up
  2. Consignment Issue
  3. Consignment Pick-Up
  4. Consignment Return

Across all four steps, the solution distinguishes between:

  • header item categories, which define the business role of the kit header
  • subitem item categories, which define the execution role of the components

This distinction is essential. The different item categories are not technical duplicates. They exist because the business meaning of the kit header is not the same in Fill-Up, Issue, Pick-Up, and Return.

Overall Processing Logic

The following table summarizes the end-to-end logic of CPFH in consignment.

Table 1: CPFH in Consignment – Overall Logic

Business Step Header Item Category Header Meaning Subitem Item Category Subitem Meaning Schedule Line Movement Type
Fill-UpCF3HKit header, preserves kit semantics, not used as a normal revenue-relevant billing itemCF3IComponents moved into customer consignment stockE1 / E0631
IssueCPHDPriced and billing-relevant kit headerCI3LComponents actually consumed / issuedC1 / C0633
IssueCFHDNot priced on header, but still billing-relevantCI3LComponents actually consumed / issuedC1 / C0633
IssueCNHDNon-priced, non-billing header, used only to preserve kit structureCI3FComponents actually consumed / issuedC1 / C0633
Pick-UpCP3HKit header for pick-up processingCP3IComponents picked up from customer consignment stockF1 / F0632
ReturnCR3PPriced and billing-relevant return headerCR3LComponents processed in returnD0634
ReturnCR3FNot priced on header, but still billing-relevantCR3LComponents processed in returnD0634
ReturnCR3NNon-priced, non-billing header, used only to preserve kit structureCR3IComponents processed in returnD0634

This table highlights the key principle of the solution:

  • the header categories differentiate the business role of the kit header
  • the subitem categories carry the actual execution logic
  • the stock-relevant processing always happens on subitem level

Consignment Fill-Up

Business Meaning

In Consignment Fill-Up, stock is moved to customer consignment stock.
At this stage, the customer has not yet actually consumed the goods. Therefore, Fill-Up is not a revenue event. It is much closer to a stock transfer into customer consignment stock.

That business meaning is reflected in the item category design.

Header: CF3H

The CPFH header item category in Fill-Up is CF3H.

Its purpose is to:

  • preserve the Sales Kit header in the document
  • keep the commercial representation of the kit
  • provide the structural basis for the exploded subitems

At the same time, CF3H is not the execution-relevant stock item. And because Fill-Up is not the point of revenue realization, it is not intended for normal billing logic. In practice, it fits pro forma invoice semantics rather than standard billing.

So the role of CF3H is not to execute the stock movement, but to keep the kit visible and meaningful in the process.

Subitem: CF3I

The actual execution in Fill-Up happens on CF3I.

CF3I represents:

  • the exploded component line
  • the material actually moved into customer consignment stock
  • the execution-relevant line for Fill-Up

It is mapped to:

  • E1 / E0
  • movement type 631

So the Fill-Up logic can be summarized as:

CF3H keeps the kit header. CF3I performs the actual fill-up execution.

Consignment Issue

Issue is the most commercially relevant step in consignment, because this is the point where the customer actually consumes stock from customer consignment inventory.

This is why the system distinguishes between multiple header item categories for Issue. They are not redundant. They reflect different business meanings of the kit header.

CPHD – Priced and Billing-Relevant Issue Header

CPHD represents the most direct commercial Issue path.

Its meaning is:

  • the header is priced
  • the header is billing-relevant
  • the kit has entered a commercial settlement path

The corresponding execution-relevant subitem is CI3L.

So CPHD + CI3L represents:

A commercially relevant kit header combined with component-level issue execution.

CFHD – Not Priced on Header, but Still Billing-Relevant

CFHD differs from CPHD.

Here, the header does not behave like a standard priced header item, but it still remains billing-relevant. This makes CFHD an alternative Issue path where commercial relevance is preserved, but header pricing is not the defining characteristic.

The corresponding subitem is also CI3L.

CNHD – Non-Priced and Non-Billing Issue Header

CNHD is especially important to understand.

Its role is:

  • no pricing
  • no billing
  • the header exists only to preserve the structural semantics of the kit

This means CNHD is not a commercial settlement header. It is a structural header. The document still needs a valid kit header, even though the actual execution happens on the components.

The corresponding subitem is CI3F.

So CNHD + CI3F represents:

A purely structural Issue header with actual component-level execution.

Subitems in Issue: CI3L and CI3F

The actual Issue execution is always done on the subitems, not on the header.

The relevant subitem categories are:

  • CI3L
  • CI3F

Both are mapped to:

  • C1 / C0
  • movement type 633

Their common role is clear:

They carry the actual component-level consumption of consignment stock.

Consignment Pick-Up

Consignment Pick-Up retrieves stock from customer consignment stock that has not yet been consumed.

Again, the business document keeps the kit header, but execution happens on the components.

Header: CP3H

CP3H is the CPFH header item category for Pick-Up.

Its purpose is to:

  • preserve the kit structure in the document
  • keep the kit header visible in pick-up processing
  • provide the structural basis for the component lines

Subitem: CP3I

The actual Pick-Up execution happens on CP3I.

It represents:

  • the components being retrieved
  • the stock-relevant line in pick-up processing

It is mapped to:

  • F1 / F0
  • movement type 632

So Pick-Up follows the same overall principle:

CP3H preserves the kit meaning, while CP3I performs the actual component-level retrieval.

Consignment Return

Return processing again differentiates between multiple header paths, because not every return header has the same commercial meaning.

CR3P – Priced and Billing-Relevant Return Header

CR3P represents:

  • a priced header
  • a billing-relevant return path
  • a commercially relevant return header for the kit

The corresponding subitem is CR3L.

So CR3P + CR3L means:

A commercially relevant return header with actual component-level return execution.

CR3F – Not Priced on Header, but Still Billing-Relevant

CR3F is conceptually similar to CFHD in Issue.

Its meaning is:

  • the header is not priced in the normal way
  • but it remains billing-relevant

The corresponding subitem is also CR3L.

CR3N – Non-Priced and Non-Billing Return Header

CR3N is the Return counterpart of CNHD.

Its role is:

  • no pricing
  • no billing
  • the header exists only to preserve the structural semantics of the kit

The corresponding execution-relevant subitem is CR3I.

So CR3N + CR3I means:

A purely structural return header with actual component-level return execution.

Subitems in Return: CR3L and CR3I

The actual Return execution happens on:

  • CR3L
  • CR3I

These are mapped to:

  • D0
  • movement type 634

So even in Return, the business pattern remains unchanged:

The header keeps the kit meaning, while the subitem performs the actual return execution.

Relationship to Standard Consignment Item Categories

A helpful way to understand the design is to compare the CPFH subitems with the standard consignment item categories.

Table 2: Standard vs. CPFH Execution Paths

Standard Item Category Business Step CPFH Subitem Item Category Meaning
CFNFill-UpCF3IStandard fill-up item vs. CPFH component fill-up item
CIN / CCIN / CCXNIssueCI3L / CI3FStandard issue item vs. CPFH component issue item
CPNPick-UpCP3IStandard pick-up item vs. CPFH component pick-up item
CRN / CRBN / CCRNReturnCR3L / CR3IStandard return item vs. CPFH component return item

This comparison makes one design principle very clear:

The solution does not create a new consignment execution model. It adds a header/subitem split on top of the standard consignment execution patterns.

In other words:

  • for a normal material, the system directly uses the standard item category path
  • for a CPFH kit, the system keeps the kit header and lets the subitems reuse the standard execution behavior

Technical Design Perspective

From a technical point of view, the implementation can be understood in three layers.

1. Item Category Determination

The system first determines the header item category, and then derives the subitem item category based on the higher-level item category.

This allows the document to preserve the kit structure while still generating execution-relevant component lines.

2. Business Role of Header and Subitem

The header item categories distinguish whether the kit header is:

  • priced
  • billing-relevant
  • or purely structural

The subitem item categories distinguish the actual execution role of the components for:

  • Fill-Up
  • Issue
  • Pick-Up
  • Return

3. Schedule Line Determination and Movement Execution

The subitems are then mapped back to the standard consignment execution framework:

  • Fill-Up: E1 / E0 → 631
  • Issue: C1 / C0 → 633
  • Pick-Up: F1 / F0 → 632
  • Return: D0 → 634

So the real innovation of the solution is not a new movement model. The innovation is:

making the CPFH kit structure executable within the standard consignment framework.

Roadmap Outlook

With the delivery of CPFH support in consignment processing in 2402, the first step has been taken to enable Sales Kits in this area.

Support for additional Sales Kit types, including ERLA, DISH, and LUMF, is planned to be introduced gradually in future releases after 2702, further expanding the overall coverage of Sales Kits in consignment scenarios.

Conclusion

Supporting CPFH in consignment is not just about allowing a Sales Kit in a consignment order. It addresses a more fundamental business and technical requirement:

How can a Sales Kit remain a kit from a commercial perspective, while still being executed consistently on component level in consignment processing?

The answer is based on three principles:

  1. The header keeps the kit semantics
    Different header item categories distinguish whether the header is priced, billing-relevant, or purely structural.
  2. The subitems carry the execution semantics
    The real inventory and movement-relevant processing happens on the component-level subitems.
  3. The execution layer reuses the standard consignment framework
    Standard schedule line categories and movement types are reused, so CPFH can run consistently within existing consignment processing.

With the 2402 delivery, CPFH Sales Kits can now be processed in consignment while preserving both:

  • the commercial representation of the kit
  • the execution logic of the components

This also lays the groundwork for further support of additional Sales Kit types in consignment scenarios in future releases.

entry.title: Fresh Look & Translatable: Our Click-Through Tutorials Got a Makeover
entry.content:
Our click-through tutorials have long been a helpful companion for navigating SAP Central Business Configuration. Now, they’ve just become even better. We’re excited to share a set of improvements designed to make your learning experience more modern and intuitive.

Design Upgrade

The click-through tutorials have received a major design overhaul. The new, refreshed look feels more modern and state of the art, making it easier and more enjoyable to follow each step. The upgraded design aligns with the latest SAP design language, ensuring a consistent user experience across SAP solutions. Whether you're exploring a process for the first time or using the tutorials as a quick refresher, the cleaner layout helps keep the focus exactly where you need it.

The new tutorial start page

Machine Translation

Did you know there’s a built-in translate feature? If English isn’t your preferred language, you can automatically translate all tutorial texts with a single click. Just select the translate icon in the top-right corner and choose from a wide range of languages – German, French, Spanish, Japanese, and many more.

Please note that all translated texts are machine generated. As such, SAP cannot provide any warranty regarding their accuracy or completeness. We appreciate your understanding and hope this feature makes the tutorials more accessible for users around the world.

Machine translation feature

Tutorial Access

There are two easy ways to access the click-through tutorials. In SAP Central Business Configuration, choose the question mark icon in the upper-right corner to open the in-app help and navigate to Learning. Alternatively, you can access the tutorials via this SAP Help Portal page.

Did you like this blog? If so, please leave us a thumbs up at the top. Also, make sure to follow the SAP Central Business Configuration tag or follow this dedicated blog collection to stay up to date with the latest blog posts.

Do you have any ideas on how to further enhance SAP Central Business Configuration? Go to the Customer Influence platform to submit an improvement request or vote for the ideas of others. This helps us understand your requirements even better and prioritize our work accordingly. We’re looking forward to your improvement requests.
